items.title: Regarding Shawn Kim: I heard from several sources that he made bearish comments on memory during a call. One source also told me that he made similar remarks during a roadshow. Another source said that he remains bearish on memory and had only temporarily softened his stance in response to market conditions. I was even told that his view differs significantly from that of the North American team. To clarify the situation, two notes were circulated in Korea today, both purportedly from Sean Kim. One was actually from 2022 and had been edited to make it appear as though Sean Kim’s report had been released today, triggering panic selling. The other was the one I posted earlier. However, I delet
items.content:
Regarding Shawn Kim:

I heard from several sources that he made bearish comments on memory during a call.

One source also told me that he made similar remarks during a roadshow.

Another source said that he remains bearish on memory and had only temporarily softened his stance in response to market conditions. I was even told that his view differs significantly from that of the North American team.

To clarify the situation, two notes were circulated in Korea today, both purportedly from Sean Kim. One was actually from 2022 and had been edited to make it appear as though Sean Kim’s report had been released today, triggering panic selling.

The other was the one I posted earlier. However, I deleted it because I had verified it based on only one source and therefore could not vouch for its accuracy.

I apologize for the confusion.
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 24, 2026

items.title: RT by @jukan05: RT by @jukan05: Jukan is right. Korea has seen some weakness recently. A certain Korean equity analyst has also expressed a cautious view, drawing much of his research from information in the Chinese market. This pattern has persisted for several quarters now — this quarter is certainly not the first time. This is largely because China’s inference demand has yet to take off, while the smartphone market there remains concentrated in lower-end models. However, you can also see that his US-based colleagues, the reports we have published, the detailed notes in our latest interview database, as well as surveys such as the most recent Cleveland Research survey, all indicate that th
items.content:
Jukan is right. Korea has seen some weakness recently. A certain Korean equity analyst has also expressed a cautious view, drawing much of his research from information in the Chinese market. This pattern has persisted for several quarters now — this quarter is certainly not the first time. This is largely because China’s inference demand has yet to take off, while the smartphone market there remains concentrated in lower-end models. 
However, you can also see that his US-based colleagues, the reports we have published, the detailed notes in our latest interview database, as well as surveys such as the most recent Cleveland Research survey, all indicate that the US has broadly accepted a new round of price increases, with the scope of the increases already negotiated and agreed upon. 
This has also made China's influence on pricing increasingly limited, and in reality it is not nearly as significant as this Korean equity analyst believes. 
The analyst says Americans are too bullish, but that's simply because the reality is exactly that. If China's inference market were to improve substantially, Chinese buyers would change their stance as well.
— @FundaAI Jul 24, 2026

items.title: RT by @jukan05: RT by @jukan05: Jukan is right. I think the recent decline in Korea is indeed highly correlated with the views of a certain Korean equity analyst. Much of his research is drawn from information in the Chinese market, which has always been a highly tactical market, and this kind of tactical behavior has been going on for several quarters now — this quarter is certainly not the first time. A large part of the reason is that China's inference demand has yet to take off, and phones there are also predominantly low-end models. However, you can also see that his US-based colleagues, the reports we have published, the detailed notes in our latest interview database, as well as surve
items.content:
Jukan is right. I think the recent decline in Korea is indeed highly correlated with the views of a certain Korean equity analyst. Much of his research is drawn from information in the Chinese market, which has always been a highly tactical market, and this kind of tactical behavior has been going on for several quarters now — this quarter is certainly not the first time. A large part of the reason is that China's inference demand has yet to take off, and phones there are also predominantly low-end models. 
However, you can also see that his US-based colleagues, the reports we have published, the detailed notes in our latest interview database, as well as surveys such as the most recent Cleveland Research survey, all indicate that the US has broadly accepted a new round of price increases, with the scope of the increases already negotiated and agreed upon. 
This has also made China's influence on pricing increasingly limited, and in reality it is not nearly as significant as this Korean equity analyst believes. 
The analyst says Americans are too bullish, but that's simply because the reality is exactly that. If China's inference market were to improve substantially, Chinese buyers would change their stance as well.
— @FundaAI Jul 24, 2026

items.title: Why the KOSPI is down today: A note is circulating that Morgan Stanley analyst Shawn Kim has turned bearish on memory. Summary of the note: ---------------------------------------------------------- Shawn Kim’s bearish take on memory - NAND Module makers are holding about 13 weeks of inventory, compared with a COVID-era peak of 15 weeks.“Demand has evaporated.” - The pace of price increases is expected to slow to 5% QoQ in Q4, reducing the need to pull forward demand.The opportunity cost of pulling forward demand is also much lower. - CSPs say their inventory levels are healthy.Tencent, one of the CSPs, said it has already secured 90% of its requirements. - NAND spot prices have been declini
items.content:
Why the KOSPI is down today:

A note is circulating that Morgan Stanley analyst Shawn Kim has turned bearish on memory.

Summary of the note:
----------------------------------------------------------
Shawn Kim’s bearish take on memory

- NAND
Module makers are holding about 13 weeks of inventory, compared with a COVID-era peak of 15 weeks.“Demand has evaporated.”

- The pace of price increases is expected to slow to 5% QoQ in Q4, reducing the need to pull forward demand.The opportunity cost of pulling forward demand is also much lower.

- CSPs say their inventory levels are healthy.Tencent, one of the CSPs, said it has already secured 90% of its requirements.
- NAND spot prices have been declining over the past two months.

- Demand and supply appear to be converging.CXMT is ramping up supply rapidly.

- DRAM: If NAND goes down, sell DRAM as well.

- HBM: Growth is expected to be capped at around 40%, reportedly based on TrendForce data.

- Joe Moore’s checks: Joe Moore looked into why their views differ.

- His checks were conducted with U.S. CSPs, which may be overly bullish on AI.
----------------------------------------------------------
I checked with sources and confirmed that the note is genuine. Shawn Kim has reportedly been expressing views along these lines recently. This may have been one of the factors behind the KOSPI’s pullback over the past few weeks.
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 24, 2026

items.title: Take a guess which North American customer is collaborating with SK hynix. My guess is Apple. By the way, I believe this stacked DRAM technology needs to come first before on-device AI can truly take off. We’re still a long way from the widespread adoption of on-device AI—unless they plan to put 128GB of LPDDR in a smartphone, that is.
items.content:
Take a guess which North American customer is collaborating with SK hynix.

My guess is Apple.

By the way, I believe this stacked DRAM technology needs to come first before on-device AI can truly take off. We’re still a long way from the widespread adoption of on-device AI—unless they plan to put 128GB of LPDDR in a smartphone, that is.Jukan (@jukan05)SK hynix Begins Development of “3D-Stacked DRAM” for On-Device AI

SK hynix is accelerating the development of 3D-stacked DRAM, regarded as a next-generation memory solution for on-device AI. The company is reportedly recruiting engineers to jointly develop the technology with a U.S. customer.

The 3D-stacked DRAM-on-logic architecture is a next-generation semiconductor technology that stacks memory directly on top of a logic, or system semiconductor, chip. It is expected to serve as a key component in edge AI devices operating on-device as the industry moves beyond generative AI and into the era of physical AI.

According to industry sources on the 24th, SK hynix has begun recruiting 3D-stacked DRAM-on-logic design engineers through its U.S. subsidiary in San Jose, California.

This is the first time SK hynix has recruited specialists in 3D-stacked DRAM. As the technology gains attention as a next-generation memory solution for on-device AI, the move is seen as an effort to prepare proactively for commercialization.

SK hynix is also understood to have already formed a partnership with a specific customer to apply the technology.

“We are looking for an engineer who can lead the co-design of a 3D-stacked DRAM logic die in close collaboration with a U.S. customer,” the company said in the job posting. “Our goal is to provide die-design solutions that meet evolving technological requirements and market demand.”

More specifically, SK hynix is seeking engineers to design 3D-stacked DRAM-on-logic products. This cutting-edge integration technology vertically stacks DRAM directly on top of a system semiconductor.

Compared with package-on-package, or PoP, technology—which simply places two chips one above the other—the architecture can significantly shorten the distance between chips and provide a greater number of data-transfer channels, or I/Os, within the same footprint. This reduces data-transfer latency while improving power efficiency and space utilization.

According to SK hynix, the technology is being developed primarily for the on-device AI market. On-device AI requires both high memory bandwidth and low power consumption, and concerns have been raised that PoP architectures may be unable to deliver the necessary performance.

Mobile application processors, or APs, are considered among the most promising applications. A mobile AP is the system semiconductor that acts as the brain of a smartphone. It is also an area in which the most advanced foundry and packaging processes are adopted to implement on-device AI. Apple and Qualcomm are leading companies in mobile AP design.

Speaking at the TSMC Technology Symposium 2026 in April, SK hynix Chief Development Officer Ahn Hyun said, “The integration of memory and logic technologies will provide a technological breakthrough that overcomes the limitations of conventional architectures and maximizes AI performance.”

“Beyond custom high-bandwidth memory, or HBM, we will offer solutions optimized for customers’ diverse workloads, including high-bandwidth flash, or HBF, and 3D-stacked DRAM-on-logic,” he added.—  https://x.com/jukan05/status/2080477408478769186 
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 24, 2026

items.content:
SK hynix Begins Development of “3D-Stacked DRAM” for On-Device AI

SK hynix is accelerating the development of 3D-stacked DRAM, regarded as a next-generation memory solution for on-device AI. The company is reportedly recruiting engineers to jointly develop the technology with a U.S. customer.

The 3D-stacked DRAM-on-logic architecture is a next-generation semiconductor technology that stacks memory directly on top of a logic, or system semiconductor, chip. It is expected to serve as a key component in edge AI devices operating on-device as the industry moves beyond generative AI and into the era of physical AI.

According to industry sources on the 24th, SK hynix has begun recruiting 3D-stacked DRAM-on-logic design engineers through its U.S. subsidiary in San Jose, California.

This is the first time SK hynix has recruited specialists in 3D-stacked DRAM. As the technology gains attention as a next-generation memory solution for on-device AI, the move is seen as an effort to prepare proactively for commercialization.

SK hynix is also understood to have already formed a partnership with a specific customer to apply the technology.

“We are looking for an engineer who can lead the co-design of a 3D-stacked DRAM logic die in close collaboration with a U.S. customer,” the company said in the job posting. “Our goal is to provide die-design solutions that meet evolving technological requirements and market demand.”

More specifically, SK hynix is seeking engineers to design 3D-stacked DRAM-on-logic products. This cutting-edge integration technology vertically stacks DRAM directly on top of a system semiconductor.

Compared with package-on-package, or PoP, technology—which simply places two chips one above the other—the architecture can significantly shorten the distance between chips and provide a greater number of data-transfer channels, or I/Os, within the same footprint. This reduces data-transfer latency while improving power efficiency and space utilization.

According to SK hynix, the technology is being developed primarily for the on-device AI market. On-device AI requires both high memory bandwidth and low power consumption, and concerns have been raised that PoP architectures may be unable to deliver the necessary performance.

Mobile application processors, or APs, are considered among the most promising applications. A mobile AP is the system semiconductor that acts as the brain of a smartphone. It is also an area in which the most advanced foundry and packaging processes are adopted to implement on-device AI. Apple and Qualcomm are leading companies in mobile AP design.

Speaking at the TSMC Technology Symposium 2026 in April, SK hynix Chief Development Officer Ahn Hyun said, “The integration of memory and logic technologies will provide a technological breakthrough that overcomes the limitations of conventional architectures and maximizes AI performance.”

“Beyond custom high-bandwidth memory, or HBM, we will offer solutions optimized for customers’ diverse workloads, including high-bandwidth flash, or HBF, and 3D-stacked DRAM-on-logic,” he added.
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 24, 2026

items.title: Great. Would you trust DigiTimes or SemiAnalysis?
items.content:
Great. Would you trust DigiTimes or SemiAnalysis?Jukan (@jukan05)▶ Nvidia 800V HVDC to Enter Production in 1Q27; Delta Poised for 2Q Ramp-Up

- SemiAnalysis has raised concerns over growth in the AI power supply chain, forecasting that the large-scale adoption of Nvidia’s 800V HVDC power architecture could be delayed from the previously expected 2027 timeline to 2028 or later.

- Supply-chain sources argue that the market has misinterpreted both the cause of the delay and the mass-production schedule. The issue relates to delays in validating first-generation product designs, rather than a change in the technology roadmap.

- Power cabinets, battery backup units (BBUs), battery packs, and related components have been resubmitted for validation, which is reportedly progressing smoothly.

- Delta Electronics is likely to begin low-volume production of 800V HVDC products toward the end of 1Q27 and gradually ramp up shipments from 2Q27 in line with customer deployment schedules.

- The main cause of the delay is product design validation rather than the 800V HVDC technology itself. A potential short-circuit risk was identified during initial system validation, prompting U.S. customers to request additional testing.

- This extended the system redesign and customer certification timelines, leading Delta Electronics, Simplo Technology, and upstream material suppliers to adjust their delivery schedules.

- Supply-chain sources emphasize that the issue is not whether the products can be manufactured, but whether design modifications and validation can be completed. They maintain that the long-term transition toward 800V HVDC as the mainstream power architecture for AI data centers remains intact.

- They also argue that the view that 800V HVDC has become less necessary because the Rubin platform still uses a 50V power structure is based on a misunderstanding. The 50V architecture handles power distribution within the rack, while 800V HVDC supplies high-voltage power from the data center to the rack.

- Rack-level power consumption is expected to increase to 600kW–1MW with Rubin Ultra and subsequent platforms. Conventional AC and low-voltage architectures will face challenges including excessive current, transmission losses, and cooling constraints.

- During the initial adoption phase, ±400V HVDC and 800V HVDC are expected to coexist. The ±400V architecture will serve as a transitional option, while 800V penetration should expand as platforms evolve and product validation is completed.

- Although the adoption of 800V HVDC has been slightly delayed relative to previous expectations, this is viewed as a timing shift in shipments rather than a deterioration in underlying demand.

- Official production is expected to begin toward the end of 1Q27, followed by a gradual ramp-up from 2Q27. From 2027 onward, increased investment by major cloud service providers and upgrades to AI data-center power infrastructure are expected to drive a new growth cycle for Taiwanese power-supply and related component manufacturers.—  https://x.com/jukan05/status/2080426188485575026 
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 23, 2026

The issue relates to delays in validating first-generation product designs, rather than a change in the technology roadmap. - Power cabinets, battery backup units (BBUs), battery packs, and related components have been resubmitted for validation, which is repo
items.content:
▶ Nvidia 800V HVDC to Enter Production in 1Q27; Delta Poised for 2Q Ramp-Up

- SemiAnalysis has raised concerns over growth in the AI power supply chain, forecasting that the large-scale adoption of Nvidia’s 800V HVDC power architecture could be delayed from the previously expected 2027 timeline to 2028 or later.

- Supply-chain sources argue that the market has misinterpreted both the cause of the delay and the mass-production schedule. The issue relates to delays in validating first-generation product designs, rather than a change in the technology roadmap.

- Power cabinets, battery backup units (BBUs), battery packs, and related components have been resubmitted for validation, which is reportedly progressing smoothly.

- Delta Electronics is likely to begin low-volume production of 800V HVDC products toward the end of 1Q27 and gradually ramp up shipments from 2Q27 in line with customer deployment schedules.

- The main cause of the delay is product design validation rather than the 800V HVDC technology itself. A potential short-circuit risk was identified during initial system validation, prompting U.S. customers to request additional testing.

- This extended the system redesign and customer certification timelines, leading Delta Electronics, Simplo Technology, and upstream material suppliers to adjust their delivery schedules.

- Supply-chain sources emphasize that the issue is not whether the products can be manufactured, but whether design modifications and validation can be completed. They maintain that the long-term transition toward 800V HVDC as the mainstream power architecture for AI data centers remains intact.

- They also argue that the view that 800V HVDC has become less necessary because the Rubin platform still uses a 50V power structure is based on a misunderstanding. The 50V architecture handles power distribution within the rack, while 800V HVDC supplies high-voltage power from the data center to the rack.

- Rack-level power consumption is expected to increase to 600kW–1MW with Rubin Ultra and subsequent platforms. Conventional AC and low-voltage architectures will face challenges including excessive current, transmission losses, and cooling constraints.

- During the initial adoption phase, ±400V HVDC and 800V HVDC are expected to coexist. The ±400V architecture will serve as a transitional option, while 800V penetration should expand as platforms evolve and product validation is completed.

- Although the adoption of 800V HVDC has been slightly delayed relative to previous expectations, this is viewed as a timing shift in shipments rather than a deterioration in underlying demand.

- Official production is expected to begin toward the end of 1Q27, followed by a gradual ramp-up from 2Q27. From 2027 onward, increased investment by major cloud service providers and upgrades to AI data-center power infrastructure are expected to drive a new growth cycle for Taiwanese power-supply and related component manufacturers.
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 23, 2026

items.title: Wall Street needs to own up to all the bullshit it was spewing about 14A entering high-volume production in 2029…
items.content:
Wall Street needs to own up to all the bullshit it was spewing about 14A entering high-volume production in 2029…Ben Bajarin (@BenBajarin)From the  $INTC call.  Particularly the last sentence.  

FINALLY they said it lol. 

"We made the decision in Q2 to fully committed to high volume ramp in 2028"

"Our early work on Intel 14A continues to make good progress. We continue to build out and validate the IP portfolio for Intel 14A as we position the 14A family for broad adoption across a wide range of customers.
I'm pleased to see the increasing momentum in customer engagements for Intel 14A, and I'm increasingly confident that 14A will be a highly competitive process technology across the key vectors of performance, power, density, cost, and schedule.
With encouraging progress from external customers and increasing demand from our internal product teams, we remain on track for Intel 14A risk production for internal products in the second half of 2027 and we made the decision in Q2 to fully committed to high volume ramp in 2028"—  https://x.com/BenBajarin/status/2080402853752107349 
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 23, 2026
